YOUR OFE 2025 REPORT

Author

Farmers DataLab

Published

April 13, 2026

2025 Cropping Season Field Ntrials  |  N rates: 0, 30, 90, 150 lb N/ac  |  Research question: Can applying cover crops reduce the amount of nitrogen required to maximize grain yields?
⬇ Download PDF

Welcome to your 2025 farm report. This report summarizes the on-farm nitrogen rate trial conducted in the Ntrials field. Four nitrogen rates were tested: 0 (Zero), 30 (Low), 90 (Standard rate), and 150 (High) lb N/ac, in a randomized complete block design with 4 blocks. Statistical comparisons use a randomized complete block design (RCBD) ANOVA with Tukey post-hoc tests. Letters in the figures indicate treatment groups that are significantly different from each other (p < 0.05).

🗺️ Open Interactive NDVI Map

Take-home message: Take-home message: No matter how much nitrogen we applied, from zero all the way up to 150 lb/ac, yield, plant color, and end-of-season stalk nitrate were virtually identical across all strips. Your soil appears to be supplying nitrogen on its own, well beyond what the crop needed this season. Based on this data, you may be applying more nitrogen than necessary. Continuing with cover crops remains a smart long-term investment to keep building soil health, even if the short-term nitrogen response isn’t showing up yet.

Results Summary

Metric ANOVA p-value Zero (0 lb N) Low (30 lb N) Standard (90 lb N) High (150 lb N)
🌾 Corn Yield (bu/ac) p = 0.593 163.5 164.9 153.8 167.7
🌿 NBI (Nitrogen Balance Index) p = 0.898 86.7 87 91.6 85.7
🍃 Chlorophyll Index p = 0.859 46 48.2 48.1 48
🧪 Biomass %N (C:N analysis) p = 0.764 4.1 4.2 4.1 4.2
🌽 CSNT (Cornstalk Nitrate) p = 1 4588 4778 4818 4629

Means shown. Letters from Tukey HSD test shown in individual section figures. ANOVA: RCBD, 4 blocks × 4 treatments.

🌾 Corn Yield

We measured corn yield in each strip and matched it to the nitrogen (N) rate used. Then we compared the average yields across the four N rates (0, 30, 90, and 150 lb N) to see if any rate clearly outperformed the others. We also grouped the results to show which N rates are truly different from each other, versus differences that are just normal field variation (block design-RCBD). Strips sharing the same letter are statistically the same (Tukey HSD). Finally, we tested whether yield increased steadily with more N applied (linear regression).

Zero vs High (150 lb N/ac)
163.5 ↑ +2.6% to 167.7 bu/ac
from 0 to 150 lb N/ac applied
ANOVA p-value
p = 0.593
Linear regression R² = 0 · p = 0.942
— No significant difference detected
  • Mean ± SE + Tukey
  • Raw data by strip

🌿 Nitrogen Balance Index (NBI)

The Nitrogen Balance Index (NBI) is measured with the Dualex sensor and gives an idea of how much nitrogen the plant has. It compares leaf greenness to stress signals in the leaf, so higher numbers mean the plant has enough nitrogen. We took two leaf readings per plant and averaged them. Then we compared the average NBI across the four N rates (0, 30, 90, and 150 lb N) to see if adding more nitrogen improved plant status. We also grouped the results to show which rates are truly different, versus differences that are just normal field variation (block design-RCBD). Strips sharing the same letter are statistically the same(Tukey HSD).

Zero vs High (150 lb N/ac)
86.7 ↓ -1.2% to 85.7
from 0 to 150 lb N/ac applied
ANOVA p-value
p = 0.898
RCBD ANOVA across Zero, Low, Standard, High
— No significant difference detected
  • Density
  • Mean ± SE + Tukey
  • Boxplot (raw data)

🍃 Chlorophyll (Chl)

The Chlorophyll Index (Chl) estimates chlorophyll content in the leaf, a reliable proxy for nitrogen sufficiency. Greener leaves generally mean more nitrogen in the plant. As with NBI, two leaves per plant were averaged. We also grouped the results to show which rates are truly different, versus differences that are just normal field variation (block design-RCBD). Strips sharing the same letter are statistically the same(Tukey HSD).

Zero vs High (150 lb N/ac)
46 ↑ +4.4% to 48
from 0 to 150 lb N/ac applied
ANOVA p-value
p = 0.859
RCBD ANOVA across Zero, Low, Standard, High
— No significant difference detected
  • Density
  • Mean ± SE + Tukey
  • Boxplot (raw data)

🧪 C:N in Corn Biomass

We collected corn plant samples from each to measure how much nitrogen actually ended up inside the plant tissue. This is one of the most direct measurements we have, it tells you what the crop actually absorbed, not just what was available in the soil. Each of the 16 plots had two sub-samples averaged together, and we used the same block design as the other analyses to keep the comparison fair. We also ran a linear regression to see if tissue nitrogen climbed steadily with more N applied.

Zero vs High (150 lb N/ac)
4.1 ↑ +2.1% to 4.2 %N
from 0 to 150 lb N/ac applied
ANOVA p-value
p = 0.764
Linear regression R² = 0.003 · p = 0.782
— No significant difference detected
  • Mean ± SE + Tukey
  • Raw data points

🌽 Cornstalk Nitrate Test (CSNT)

The Cornstalk Nitrate Test (CSNT) measures nitrate concentration in the lower cornstalk at the end of the season. It is a post-harvest diagnostic tool: values below 250 ppm indicate insufficient nitrogen, while the optimal range is between 750 and 2000 ppm. Each plot had two samples (E and W positions), giving 32 observations across the 16 plots. The red dashed line marks the 250 ppm deficiency threshold and the green line the 750 ppm optimal threshold.

Zero vs High (150 lb N/ac)
4588 ↑ +0.9% to 4629 ppm
from 0 to 150 lb N/ac applied
ANOVA p-value
p = 1
RCBD ANOVA — E and W samples averaged per plot
— No significant difference detected
  • Mean ± SE + Tukey
  • Raw data by plot

🌱 Soil Health Assessment

Soil health was assessed using the Cornell Soil Health Test (sampled 04/30/2025, Ntrials field). The overall quality score was 57/100 (Medium). The biological indicators are the main area of concern: soil respiration, organic matter, active carbon, and soil protein index all came back Low. These are the indicators that reflect how active and alive your soil biology is, and right now, that biological engine is running at a lower capacity than ideal. The interesting thing is that despite these low biological scores, your field clearly had plenty of nitrogen available this season. That nitrogen is likely coming from a large existing reserve in the soil, not from active biological cycling. Cover cropping is a direct investment in improving exactly these biological scores over time, feeding the microbes, adding carbon, and building a soil that can supply nitrogen more consistently from its own internal processes.

↗ Open Full Soil Health Report (PDF)

🌧️Weather & Drought Conditions

Cumulative precipitation at your field compared to the all-site average, alongside the weekly drought index from June through November 2025. Your field tracked closely with the all-site average through the summer months, with consistent rainfall from June through August and a cumulative total near the network average heading into September. A strong rain event in late June provided a good early push for the crop. The second half of the season brought a notable dry stretch, drought conditions escalated from Low in late September to Mild for most of October, lasting roughly four to five weeks before easing in November. This late-season dry period coincided with the end of grain fill and is worth keeping in mind when interpreting end-of-season stalk nitrate results, as dry conditions can concentrate nitrate in the stalk even when overall nitrogen supply is adequate.

📝 Conclusions

🌾 Corn Yield
Yield was similar across all four N rates, ranging from 153.8 to 167.7 bu/ac. No clear pattern emerged as nitrogen increased — the strips that got zero fertilizer performed just as well as the ones that got 150 lb N/ac. The soil appears to have supplied all the nitrogen the crop needed on its own this season.
🌿 NBI
NBI values were similar across all treatments (86–92), all within a healthy range. Adding more nitrogen did not improve leaf nitrogen status, indicating plants were well-supplied regardless of N rate.
🍃 Chlorophyll
Chlorophyll was nearly identical across all N rates (46–48), confirming that leaf greenness was not affected by how much nitrogen was applied. Plants showed no signs of nitrogen stress even at zero applied N.
🧪 Biomass %N
Tissue nitrogen was consistent across treatments (4.1–4.2%), with no meaningful difference between the zero and high N rate strips. N rate had no detectable effect on how much nitrogen the plant accumulated at this growth stage.
🌽 CSNT
End-of-season stalk nitrate was very high in all treatments (4,588–4,818 ppm), well above the 750 ppm optimal threshold. The crop had abundant nitrogen at season end regardless of how much was applied.
🌱 Soil Health
The overall soil health score was 57/100 (Medium), with low biological activity across the board. Despite this, the field appears to have a large reserve of available nitrogen. Continued cover cropping could improve organic matter and microbial activity over time, even if a single-season nitrogen response is not yet visible.
